---
title: "Pima Dataset Visual Analysis and Correlations"
author: "Coding Agent"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE);
```

```{r libraries}
# Load all required libraries at the top
library(pdp);
library(randomForest);
library(ggplot2);
library(dplyr);
library(corrplot);
library(sf);
library(tigris);
library(tmap);

```

```{r load_data}
# Load the Pima dataset from pdp package
data(pima, package = "pdp");

# Remove rows with missing values for analysis
pima <- na.omit(pima);
```

```{r explore_data}
# Initial data exploration
summary(pima);
str(pima);
```

```{r split_data}
# Split data into training and testing sets as per standard practice
set.seed(123);
train_proportion <- 0.7;
train_indices <- sample(1:nrow(pima), size = floor(train_proportion * nrow(pima)));
train_data <- pima[train_indices, ];
test_data <- pima[-train_indices, ];
```

```{r correlations}
# Calculate correlations for numeric variables
numeric_columns <- sapply(pima, is.numeric);
correlation_matrix <- cor(pima[, numeric_columns], use = "pairwise.complete.obs");

# Visualize correlation matrix
corrplot(correlation_matrix, method = "circle");
```

```{r correlation_analysis}
# Find highly correlated variable pairs (correlation > 0.5)
high_correlations <- which(abs(correlation_matrix) > 0.5 & correlation_matrix != 1, arr.ind = TRUE);
high_correlations <- high_correlations[high_correlations[, 1] < high_correlations[, 2], ];  # Remove duplicates

# Display highly correlated pairs
if (nrow(high_correlations) > 0) {
  correlated_pairs <- data.frame(
    Var1 = rownames(correlation_matrix)[high_correlations[, 1]],
    Var2 = colnames(correlation_matrix)[high_correlations[, 2]],
    Correlation = correlation_matrix[high_correlations]
  );
  print(correlated_pairs);
} else {
  print("No highly correlated pairs found.");
}
```

```{r histograms}
# Create histograms for all numeric variables
numeric_vars <- names(pima)[numeric_columns];
for (variable_name in numeric_vars) {
  plot <- ggplot(pima, aes_string(x = variable_name)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "blue", alpha = 0.7) +
    labs(title = paste("Histogram of", variable_name), x = variable_name, y = "Frequency") +
    theme_minimal();
  print(plot);
}
```

```{r boxplots}
# Create boxplots for numeric variables by response variable (assuming diabetes is the response)
response_var <- "diabetes";  # The response variable
for (variable_name in numeric_vars) {
  if (variable_name != response_var) {
    plot <- ggplot(pima, aes_string(x = response_var, y = variable_name)) +
      geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue") +
      labs(title = paste("Boxplot of", variable_name, "by", response_var), x = response_var, y = variable_name) +
      theme_minimal();
    print(plot);
  }
}
```

```{r scatter_plots}
# Create scatter plots for highly correlated pairs
if (exists("correlated_pairs") && nrow(correlated_pairs) > 0) {
  for (ii in 1:nrow(correlated_pairs)) {
    var1 <- correlated_pairs$Var1[ii];
    var2 <- correlated_pairs$Var2[ii];
    plot <- ggplot(pima, aes_string(x = var1, y = var2)) +
      geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
      geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") +
      labs(title = paste("Scatter plot of", var1, "vs", var2), x = var1, y = var2) +
      theme_minimal();
    print(plot);
  }
}
```

```{r fit_model}
# Fit a random forest model for partial dependence analysis
rf_model <- randomForest(as.formula(paste(response_var, "~ .")), data = train_data);
```

```{r partial_dependence}
# Calculate partial dependence for the first predictor variable
# Assuming the first column after response is a predictor
predictor_vars <- setdiff(names(pima), response_var);
first_predictor <- predictor_vars[1];

pdp_result <- partial(rf_model, pred.var = first_predictor, train = train_data);

# Plot partial dependence
plotPartial(pdp_result, main = paste("Partial Dependence of", response_var, "on", first_predictor));
```

```{r additional_pdps}
# Calculate and plot partial dependence for other key variables
for (variable_name in predictor_vars[2:min(3, length(predictor_vars))]) {  # Plot for first 3 predictors
  pdp_result <- partial(rf_model, pred.var = variable_name, train = train_data);
  plotPartial(pdp_result, main = paste("Partial Dependence of", response_var, "on", variable_name));
}
```
